title: What is GEO? A Marketer's Guide to Generative Engine Optimisation
excerpt: Google AI Overviews, ChatGPT Search, and Perplexity AI are changing how buyers discover brands. GEO is the new discipline that gets you cited — not just ranked.
author: AKA Digital Team
body_html:
Something changed in how people search — and most marketers haven't caught up yet.

Open ChatGPT and type "best CRM platform for enterprise brands in Vietnam." You'll get a confident, cited answer within seconds. No results page. No blue links to scroll through. Just a synthesised response that names specific vendors, explains trade-offs, and may or may not include your brand.

That is the new reality of B2B buying research. And it's why Generative Engine Optimisation (GEO) has become the most important new discipline in digital marketing.

What Exactly is GEO?

Generative Engine Optimisation is the practice of structuring your brand's content, authority signals, and technical data so that AI-powered search systems — Google AI Overviews, ChatGPT Search, Perplexity AI, Microsoft Copilot, and others — cite your brand in their generated responses.

The key distinction from traditional SEO: you are not trying to rank on a results page. You are trying to be selected as a source by a language model that is writing an answer on behalf of the user. The model doesn't show 10 results — it chooses a handful of authoritative references and synthesises them into prose. If you're not one of those sources, you don't exist in that answer.

Why This Matters Right Now

The numbers are stark. Google AI Overviews now appear on over 40% of searches in markets where they've rolled out. Perplexity AI handles over 100 million queries per week. A 2024 Gartner study projected that by 2026, traditional search engine volume will drop by 25% as AI-powered interfaces absorb more of the research phase.

For B2B brands in Southeast Asia, this is particularly acute. The buyer journey for enterprise software, financial services, or professional services typically involves 5–8 touchpoints before a sales conversation. An increasing proportion of those early research moments now happen inside an AI chat interface — not Google.

If your brand is invisible in AI-generated answers during those early research moments, you're not in the consideration set before the sales conversation even starts.

How GEO Differs From SEO

Traditional SEO optimises for visibility on a ranked list. GEO optimises for inclusion in a generated answer. The underlying mechanics are meaningfully different:

DimensionTraditional SEOGEO
GoalRank #1 on a results pageBe cited in an AI-generated answer
Signal typeBacklinks, keywords, page authorityEntity clarity, content structure, citation patterns
Content formatLong-form keyword-rich pagesDirect, citable, answer-structured content
MeasurementImpressions, clicks, rank positionBrand mention rate in AI responses
Timeline3–12 months for organic resultsWeeks to see citation improvements with right signals

The 5 Pillars of GEO

1. Entity Clarity

AI systems need to know who you are with confidence before they'll cite you. This means having a consistent brand entity across Wikipedia (if applicable), Wikidata, Google Knowledge Graph, Crunchbase, LinkedIn, and major industry directories. The more clearly your brand's identity, expertise, and category are defined across these sources, the more likely an LLM is to surface you as an authority.

2. Citation-Ready Content Structure

LLMs extract information from content that is easy to parse. Short, declarative sentences. Clear H2 and H3 headers framing specific questions. Bullet-point definitions. Tables comparing options. Numbered step processes. The format that answers a question directly — not the format that tells a brand story — is what gets cited.

Audit your key pages: can a language model extract a crisp, quotable answer to a relevant question from each one within the first 200 words?

3. Authority Signal Building

AI systems weight content from sources that are already considered authoritative in a domain. This means earning mentions and citations from industry publications, conference proceedings, partner press releases, and thought-leader profiles. The goal is to appear as a subject matter authority, not just a vendor — which requires a content investment in genuinely useful expert material.

4. Answer Engine Optimisation (AEO)

AEO is a subset of GEO focused specifically on voice search and featured snippet capture — the zero-click answer formats that feed directly into AI systems. Structuring content around natural-language questions ("What is…", "How do I…", "Which platform is best for…") and providing direct, concise answers within 40–60 words beneath each question header directly increases the probability of appearing in AI answers.

5. Technical Schema and Structured Data

Schema.org markup — particularly Organization, Product, FAQPage, HowTo, and Article schemas — gives AI systems machine-readable context about what your content means. This is the technical layer that many brands skip. It's also one of the highest-leverage GEO improvements you can implement in a single sprint.

Measuring GEO Performance

Traditional SEO metrics (impressions, rank position, organic clicks) only partially capture GEO performance. You need to measure:

  • AI citation rate — how often does your brand appear when relevant queries are run across ChatGPT, Perplexity, and Google AI Overviews?
  • Share of voice in AI answers — when your category is queried, are you mentioned ahead of or alongside competitors?
  • Direct navigation and branded search uplift — a downstream signal that AI mentions are driving brand awareness

AKA Digital uses the Hashmeta.ai GEO platform to track brand visibility across AI search engines for our clients — running structured query sets across multiple AI platforms weekly to measure citation frequency and competitive share of voice.

Getting Started: The GEO Audit

The fastest way to understand your current GEO position is a structured audit. Run 20–30 queries relevant to your category across ChatGPT, Perplexity AI, and Google AI Overviews. Note which brands get cited and how often. Then audit your own site against the five pillars above.

Most enterprise brands in Southeast Asia have significant GEO opportunity sitting untapped — particularly in the entity clarity and content structure dimensions, where relatively small changes produce measurable citation improvements within weeks.

AKA Digital offers free GEO audits for enterprise brands across Southeast Asia. Our GEO practice covers Google AI Overviews, ChatGPT Search, Perplexity AI, Bing Copilot, and Claude — with market-specific benchmarking for Vietnam, Singapore, Indonesia, Thailand, and Korea. Book your free GEO audit.

title: CDP vs CRM: Which Does Your Brand Actually Need in 2025?
excerpt: Most brands have a CRM. Far fewer have a CDP — and many confuse the two. Here's the definitive guide to understanding the difference and deciding what your stack actually needs.
author: AKA Digital Team
body_html:
One question comes up in almost every enterprise MarTech conversation: "Do we need a CDP, or is our CRM enough?" The short answer: they solve different problems. The longer answer is what this guide is about.

What is a CRM?

A Customer Relationship Management platform (like Salesforce, Freshworks, or HubSpot) is primarily a sales and service tool. It stores contact records, manages sales pipelines, tracks interactions, and helps sales teams close deals. CRMs are excellent at managing known customers and prospects through structured workflows.

What is a CDP?

A Customer Data Platform (like mParticle, Mixpanel, or CleverTap) is primarily a marketing data unification tool. It ingests data from every digital touchpoint — websites, apps, email, ad platforms, offline events — and creates a unified customer profile across anonymous and identified users. CDPs are excellent at understanding behaviour in real time and enabling personalised, cross-channel experiences.

Key Differences at a Glance

DimensionCRMCDP
Primary userSales & Customer ServiceMarketing & Data teams
Data typesContacts, deals, notesEvents, behaviours, identities
Real-time?RarelyYes, native
Anonymous profiles?NoYes
Activation channelsEmail, phone, CRM automationsAny digital channel

When Do You Need Both?

If you're an enterprise brand running omnichannel marketing — you need both. Your CRM handles the sales relationship and post-sale service. Your CDP handles real-time personalisation, audience segmentation, and cross-channel activation for marketing.

AKA Digital specialises in implementing both — including the data integration layer that keeps them in sync. Talk to our team about your stack.

title: First-Party Data Strategy: 5 Steps to Cookieless Marketing
excerpt: Third-party cookies are dead. Brands that own their own customer data will win the next decade. Here's the 5-step playbook AKA Digital uses with enterprise clients across Southeast Asia.
author: AKA Digital Team
body_html:
Google delayed the deprecation of third-party cookies — but the direction of travel is clear. Safari and Firefox already block them by default. Increasingly, enterprise marketers who have built their own first-party data assets are seeing dramatic improvements in targeting accuracy, ad efficiency, and campaign ROI compared to those relying on purchased audiences.

Step 1: Audit Your Current Data Assets

Start with a data audit. Most brands have more first-party data than they realise — transactional data from ERP/POS systems, email lists, app user databases, loyalty programme records, website behaviour logs. The challenge is unification, not collection. Map every data source and understand the identity keys available in each.

Step 2: Implement a Unified Customer Identity

Choose a primary customer identifier (email is most common for B2C in Southeast Asia; company email + phone for B2B). Build a Customer Data Platform (CDP) that resolves identities across systems — matching an anonymous web visitor cookie ID to a known CRM contact when they sign in or make a purchase.

Step 3: Build Zero-Party Data Collection

Zero-party data is data that customers voluntarily share — preferences, interests, communication opt-ins. Build collection touchpoints: preference centres, interactive quizzes, loyalty enrolment forms, post-purchase surveys. This data is the most valuable because it reflects expressed intent — and it's completely privacy-compliant.

Step 4: Activate Your First-Party Audiences

Upload your unified customer segments to ad platforms as Customer Match lists (Google, Meta, LinkedIn, TikTok) and as Custom Audiences for email and push platforms. First-party audiences consistently outperform third-party segments by 2–4x in our client data — because they represent people who already know your brand.

Step 5: Measure and Iterate

Implement proper attribution — not just last-click Google Analytics, but multi-touch models that account for the full customer journey. Track LTV by acquisition source, not just initial conversion. The brands that invest in this infrastructure in 2025 will have an enormous data advantage by 2027.

title: Programmatic DOOH in Southeast Asia: The Complete 2025 Guide
excerpt: Digital out-of-home advertising has gone programmatic — and Southeast Asia is one of the fastest-growing markets. Here's everything enterprise marketers need to know.
author: AKA Digital Team
body_html:
Programmatic Digital Out-of-Home (pDOOH) is one of the most exciting developments in advertising in the past decade — and Southeast Asia is emerging as a global hotspot for its adoption. With over 150,000 digital screens across Vietnam, Singapore, Indonesia, Thailand, Malaysia, and the Philippines now accessible programmatically, brands can reach mass audiences in premium environments with the precision of digital targeting.

What Makes pDOOH Different from Traditional OOH?

Traditional outdoor advertising is bought in fixed placements for fixed time periods — a billboard for 4 weeks. Programmatic DOOH is bought in real-time impressions via a DSP (Demand Side Platform), with full control over when, where, and to whom your creative appears. This means:

  • Buy by time of day (morning commute vs. evening entertainment)
  • Trigger creative based on weather, sports scores, or flight departure boards
  • Pause, optimise, and reallocate budget in real time
  • Integrate with mobile retargeting — reach people on their phones after they walk past your screen

The Southeast Asia pDOOH Landscape

Vietnam has one of the fastest-growing digital screen inventories in the region, with premium placements in Ho Chi Minh City and Hanoi covering malls, airports, streets, and transit. Singapore's inventory is extremely premium-priced but reaches the highest-value B2B audiences. Indonesia's growth is rapid, with Jakarta as the centrepiece.

How AKA Digital Runs pDOOH Campaigns

We use Google DV360 and specialist pDOOH DSPs to plan, buy, and optimise screen inventory across Southeast Asia. We integrate pDOOH into broader omnichannel campaigns — using mobile location data to measure foot traffic uplift, and retargeting pixels to follow up with people who were exposed to DOOH creative.

title: KOL Marketing in Vietnam: How to Measure ROI (Not Just Reach)
excerpt: Vietnam has one of Southeast Asia's most active KOL ecosystems — but most brands measure the wrong things. Here's how to run influencer campaigns that actually impact the bottom line.
author: AKA Digital Team
body_html:
Vietnam's Key Opinion Leader (KOL) ecosystem is enormous — particularly on TikTok, YouTube, and Instagram. Vietnamese consumers trust recommendations from content creators at unusually high rates compared to regional averages. Yet most brands that invest in KOL marketing cannot answer a basic question: "Did it actually drive sales?"

The Measurement Problem

Most KOL campaign reports lead with impressions, reach, views, and engagement rate. These are awareness metrics — useful for brand tracking, but not for revenue attribution. The problem is that brands treat these as performance metrics and make investment decisions based on them.

A Framework for KOL Revenue Attribution

1. Unique discount codes — Every KOL gets a unique discount code (e.g., CREATOR10) that is tracked from use through to purchase. This gives you exact conversion data per creator.

2. UTM-tagged links — Every link in bio, story swipe-up, or video description uses a unique UTM parameter set that flows through to your analytics and CRM.

3. Pixel retargeting — Use a Meta or TikTok Pixel on your site to build a custom audience of people who visited from a specific KOL's content — then retarget them separately and measure conversion.

4. Audience authentication — Before spending a dong on a KOL, verify their audience quality. AKA Digital uses Grin.ai's audience analysis to check for fake followers, engagement pod activity, and audience demographic alignment with your target customer.

The KOL Tier Strategy

Different KOL tiers serve different purposes. Macro KOLs (1M+ followers) drive awareness and social proof. Micro KOLs (10K–100K followers) drive higher engagement and purchase intent within niche communities. Nano KOLs (<10K followers) drive extremely high trust-based conversion in tight communities — and cost a fraction of macro rates.

The best campaigns use all three tiers — with budget allocation weighted by your primary KPI.

title: The MarTech Stack for SEA Enterprise Brands in 2025
excerpt: With 10,000+ MarTech solutions globally, picking the right stack for a Southeast Asian enterprise is harder than ever. Here's AKA Digital's framework for building a stack that delivers.
author: AKA Digital Team
body_html:
Chief Marketing Officers in Southeast Asia face a paradox: they operate in some of the world's most digitally active markets (Vietnam, Indonesia, and Thailand rank among the highest globally for mobile internet time per day) — but the enterprise MarTech infrastructure lags significantly behind Europe and North America.

Why the SEA MarTech Market is Different

Three factors make SEA MarTech unique: fragmentation (12+ countries, 8+ languages, dramatically different consumer behaviours), mobile dominance (60–80% of digital engagement is on mobile, often in-app), and cash-on-delivery norms (particularly in Vietnam and Indonesia — attribution models built for Western credit-card-first markets fail here).

The Core Stack Architecture

For a regional enterprise brand operating across 3+ SEA markets, AKA Digital recommends a four-layer stack:

  • Layer 1 — Data Foundation: A CDP (mParticle or Mixpanel) that unifies customer data across all touchpoints and markets. Without this, everything else is built on sand.
  • Layer 2 — Engagement & Automation: A marketing automation platform (CleverTap, MoEngage, or Netcore Cloud) for cross-channel campaign execution — push, email, SMS, in-app, WhatsApp.
  • Layer 3 — Acquisition & Retargeting: A programmatic DSP (DV360) plus social platforms (Meta, TikTok, LinkedIn) with first-party data activation.
  • Layer 4 — Optimisation & Analytics: A/B testing (AB Tasty) + attribution (Adjust for mobile) + business intelligence (Looker Studio).

Common Mistakes to Avoid

Mistake 1: Buying too many point solutions. The average enterprise marketing team uses 14–18 MarTech tools. More than 70% of those are underutilised. Consolidate around a unified CDP rather than adding more disconnected tools.

Mistake 2: Ignoring implementation quality. 80% of MarTech failures are implementation failures, not platform failures. Invest in expert implementation (like AKA Digital) rather than trying to configure enterprise software with an internal team that doesn't specialise in it.

Mistake 3: Measuring adoption, not outcomes. The right question is not "Are our teams using the platform?" but "Has our CPA decreased? Has our LTV increased? Is our churn rate lower?"

AKA Digital has implemented and manages MarTech stacks for 150+ enterprise brands across Southeast Asia. Book a free stack review.

title: Unlocking Growth with B2B eCommerce Advisory: Key Benefits for Your Business
excerpt: In today's fast-paced digital marketplace, businesses that sell to other businesses must adapt quickly to stay competitive. B2B eCommerce advisory transforms operations by addressing unique challenges like complex pricing, large order volumes, and extended sales cycles.
author: AKA Digital Team
body_html:
In today's fast-paced digital marketplace, businesses that sell to other businesses must adapt quickly to stay competitive. B2B eCommerce advisory services help organisations optimise their online sales channels, streamline operations, and deliver superior customer experiences that drive long-term growth.

Why B2B eCommerce Advisory is Essential

Unlike B2C, B2B commerce involves complex pricing structures, large order volumes, extended sales cycles, and deep customer relationships. Advisory services bring specialised expertise that transforms these challenges into competitive advantages — delivering customised eCommerce strategies aligned with your specific business goals.

How Advisory Drives Revenue Growth

  • AI-powered product recommendations for intelligent upselling and cross-selling
  • Tiered pricing models that reward loyalty and increase average order value
  • Optimised checkout processes to reduce abandonment and accelerate cash conversion
  • Integrated CRM systems that provide complete customer visibility

Enhancing Operational Efficiency

Expert guidance accelerates order processing, reduces manual workflows through automation, improves inventory management accuracy, and enables better cross-team collaboration — all of which reduce cost-per-transaction while improving margins.

Building Stronger Customer Relationships

Data analytics enables businesses to anticipate customer needs before they arise. Loyalty programmes designed for business clients, multi-channel support systems, and flexible payment options all contribute to higher retention rates and expanded account value.

Future-Proofing Your Business

Modular, scalable platforms ensure your eCommerce infrastructure grows with your business — with multi-currency and multi-language capabilities to support regional expansion across Southeast Asia, and emerging technology integration (AI, automation) built in from day one.

Talk to AKA Digital's team about your B2B digital transformation strategy.

title: The Role of Marketing Technology Consulting Services
excerpt: Marketing technology has become a critical component for companies aiming to optimize their marketing efforts. But choosing the right tools — and using them correctly — requires expertise most in-house teams don't have.
author: AKA Digital Team
body_html:
Marketing technology, or MarTech, has become a critical component for companies aiming to optimize their marketing efforts and drive sustainable growth. Yet most organisations invest in platforms without the expertise to extract full value — leaving significant ROI on the table.

What Marketing Technology Consulting Delivers

MarTech consulting provides expert guidance on the selection, integration, and optimisation of marketing tools and platforms. Experienced consultants assess your current processes, identify gaps, and recommend solutions that boost efficiency and ROI — based on what actually works in your markets, not generic vendor recommendations.

The Business Growth Impact

  • Enhanced personalisation through integrated customer data across every touchpoint
  • Reduced manual effort via automation of repetitive marketing tasks
  • Improved budget effectiveness — spend where attribution proves impact
  • Competitive positioning in rapidly changing digital markets

Why the Consultant Role Matters

MarTech specialists bridge the gap between marketing and IT — translating business objectives into technical requirements, evaluating tools against real use cases, and training teams on best practices that are actually adopted.

Selecting the Right Partner

When choosing a MarTech consulting partner, prioritise: deep industry experience in your sector, proven platform expertise (not just general familiarity), demonstrated success in comparable markets, and a collaborative approach that transfers knowledge to your team rather than creating dependency.

AKA Digital brings over a decade of MarTech consulting experience across Southeast Asia. Book a free consultation with our team.

title: Key Tools for Product Analytics Success
excerpt: Product analytics provides the insights needed to make data-driven decisions that enhance user experience and increase retention. But choosing the right tools — and using them correctly — makes all the difference.
author: AKA Digital Team
body_html:
Product analytics provides the insights needed to make data-driven decisions that enhance user experience and increase retention. The right analytics tools enable organisations to track user behaviour in real time and segment users based on actions or demographics — directly impacting decision-making quality.

Seven Essential Features to Look For

  • Event Tracking — monitoring clicks, form submissions, feature interactions
  • User Segmentation — organising audiences by behaviour, location, or device
  • Funnel Analysis — visualising conversion steps and identifying dropout points
  • Retention Analysis — measuring recurring engagement and identifying churn patterns
  • Integration Capabilities — connecting with CRM and marketing automation platforms
  • Customisable Dashboards — reporting tailored to each team's priorities
  • Data Privacy Compliance — adherence to GDPR, PDPA, and other regional frameworks

Leading Tools in the Market

The leading product analytics platforms each have distinct strengths. Mixpanel excels at event tracking and funnel analysis — making it AKA Digital's recommended choice for mobile-first SEA markets. Amplitude brings strong behavioural and predictive analytics. GA4 provides unified web and app measurement at no cost. Heap captures all interactions automatically without manual tagging.

Implementation Best Practices

Define clear objectives before implementation — what decisions do you need the data to inform? Establish consistent tracking protocols, review findings in regular cadences (weekly for product, monthly for strategy), enable cross-team access, and invest in user training. Analytics only creates value when teams actually act on the insights.

The Future of Product Analytics

AI-powered insight generation, cross-platform data unification, real-time processing, and no-code analytics solutions are making product intelligence accessible to teams without deep data science capability. Brands that build this infrastructure now will have a compounding advantage as these tools mature.

AKA Digital is Vietnam's exclusive partner for Mixpanel. Request a free Mixpanel demo for your product team.

title: Drive Innovation with Advanced MarTech Solutions
excerpt: Advanced marketing technology tools help companies streamline efforts, gain insights into customer behaviour, and deliver personalised experiences at scale. Here's how to choose and implement them effectively.
author: AKA Digital Team
body_html:
Advanced marketing technology platforms deliver measurable competitive advantages for brands that implement them correctly. The key benefits: improved efficiency through task automation, enhanced customer insights via unified data collection, the ability to deliver tailored content based on real customer segments, and superior ROI measurement that makes every marketing dollar accountable.

How to Choose the Right MarTech Tools

Start with your business objectives, not with platform features. Common mistake: evaluating tools based on their demo experience rather than their fit with your specific data architecture, team capabilities, and market context. A platform that works brilliantly for a US SaaS company may be entirely wrong for a Vietnamese financial services brand.

Evaluate: existing technology infrastructure compatibility, real scalability (not vendor promises), total cost of ownership including implementation and training, and the quality of local or regional support.

Leveraging Data Analytics

Modern MarTech enables sophisticated customer segmentation, predictive forecasting of churn and LTV, KPI monitoring across the full marketing funnel, and A/B testing at scale. These capabilities compound over time — brands with 3 years of clean first-party data have an enormous structural advantage over those starting today.

Integration is the Make-or-Break Factor

Connected systems eliminate data fragmentation and automate workflows across CRM, email, social, and analytics. The most common failure pattern we see: organisations invest in excellent individual tools but never achieve the integration that makes them collectively valuable. Unified data architecture is not a nice-to-have — it's the entire point.

Embracing the AI-Powered Future

AI-powered solutions, centralised customer data platforms, and emerging immersive technologies are transforming what's possible in marketing. Brands that invest now — in the data infrastructure, the processes, and the expertise — will have a compounding advantage as these capabilities mature.

AKA Digital helps enterprise brands across Southeast Asia implement, integrate, and maximise their MarTech investments. Schedule a free innovation workshop.
